name: Marcus Aurelius
bio: Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher whose private journal became the Meditations — never written for publication. He ruled the empire while practicing the discipline he wrote about daily.

name: Epictetus
bio: Born enslaved, Epictetus became one of the most influential Stoic teachers. His philosophy of radical inner freedom — that no one can take your judgment from you — was forged in lived experience.
key_ideas: what is up to us vs. not up to us

name: Seneca
bio: Statesman, playwright, and philosopher who wrote some of the most readable Stoic texts in the form of letters. He navigated the court of Nero while meditating on time, death, and equanimity.
key_ideas: time as the one true wealth

name: Zeno of Citium
bio: Founder of Stoicism who taught in the Stoa Poikile — the painted porch — in Athens. He arrived in Athens as a shipwreck survivor and shaped philosophy by asking how to live well under any condition.

name: Jean-Paul Sartre
bio: French philosopher, novelist, and playwright who argued that humans are 'condemned to be free' — there is no fixed essence, only the self we make through choices. He refused the Nobel Prize in Literature.

name: Søren Kierkegaard
bio: Danish philosopher who wrote under pseudonyms to force readers to find their own truth. He described three stages of life — aesthetic, ethical, and religious — and the 'leap of faith' between them.
key_ideas: anxiety as the dizziness of freedom

name: Martin Heidegger
bio: German philosopher who asked what it means to be — not just to exist. His concept of being-toward-death and authenticity shaped existentialism, phenomenology, and later continental philosophy.
key_ideas: care as the structure of existence

name: Simone de Beauvoir
bio: French philosopher and author who applied existentialist freedom to questions of gender and oppression. Her claim that 'one is not born a woman' opened feminist philosophy to existential analysis.

name: Friedrich Nietzsche
bio: German philosopher who diagnosed the death of God and the resulting crisis of meaning in Western culture. He proposed the Übermensch and will to power as responses — not comfort, but challenge.

name: Arthur Schopenhauer
bio: German philosopher who argued that beneath appearance lies a blind, striving Will. He found solace in aesthetic experience and asceticism, influencing Nietzsche, Freud, and Wittgenstein.
key_ideas: the world as will and representation
compassion as the basis of ethics

name: Emil Cioran
bio: Romanian-born French aphorist who wrote on despair, futility, and the absurdity of existence with dark elegance. He described his work as 'a series of intimate disasters' but kept writing anyway.

name: Albert Camus
bio: French-Algerian author and philosopher who argued against both suicide and false hope in the face of life's silence. His idea of revolt — embracing life without demanding it make sense — made him an icon of defiant joy.

name: Siddhartha Gautama
bio: Prince-turned-ascetic who achieved enlightenment under the Bodhi tree after years of searching. His teaching — the Dharma — begins with suffering and leads through attention and release to liberation.

name: Nagarjuna
bio: Indian philosopher and founder of the Madhyamaka school, which argued that all phenomena lack inherent existence — emptiness (sunyata) is not void but interdependence seen clearly.

name: Dogen
bio: Japanese Zen master who founded Soto Zen and taught that zazen — sitting meditation — is itself enlightenment, not a means to it. His writing in the Shobogenzo remains among the most profound in Zen.

name: Thich Nhat Hanh
bio: Vietnamese Zen monk and peace activist who coined 'engaged Buddhism' — the practice of bringing mindfulness into social action. Exiled from Vietnam for 39 years, he continued teaching worldwide.
key_ideas: the present moment as the only moment

name: Laozi
bio: Legendary author of the Tao Te Ching — 81 short chapters on the nature of the Way. Whether a historical figure or a composite, his text on yielding, water, and emptiness has shaped Chinese thought for millennia.
key_ideas: the Tao that can be named is not the eternal Tao

name: Zhuangzi
bio: Taoist philosopher and master storyteller whose parables — the butterfly dream, the cook and the ox — teach transformation and the relativity of all fixed perspectives with surprising humor.

name: Epicurus
bio: Greek philosopher who founded a community called The Garden and taught that the good life requires only simple pleasures, friendship, and freedom from fear. Often misunderstood as hedonist, he was an advocate of tranquility.
key_ideas: the pleasure of the mind over body

name: Lucretius
bio: Roman poet-philosopher who wrote On the Nature of Things — the most complete surviving account of Epicurean physics and ethics. He argued that atoms and void are all there is, and death is therefore nothing to fear.
key_ideas: the fear of death as irrational
the plague as test of philosophy

name: Jeremy Bentham
bio: British philosopher who founded modern utilitarianism and designed the panopticon prison. He proposed measuring pleasure and pain mathematically to guide legislation, leaving his body to be preserved as an 'auto-icon.'

name: John Stuart Mill
bio: British philosopher who refined utilitarianism by distinguishing quality of pleasures and championed individual liberty. His essay On Liberty remains a foundational text for liberal political thought.
key_ideas: liberty as the foundation of flourishing

name: Peter Singer
bio: Australian philosopher and leading voice of effective altruism who argues that suffering matters regardless of species. His essay Famine, Affluence, and Morality challenges comfortable assumptions about moral duty.

name: William James
bio: American philosopher and psychologist who made pragmatism a philosophy of life. He argued that truth is what works in practice — ideas are instruments for navigating experience, not mirrors of reality.

name: John Dewey
bio: American philosopher and education reformer who argued that learning requires doing. He saw democracy as a way of life, not just a system, and philosophy as a tool for social problem-solving.
key_ideas: inquiry as the model for knowledge

name: Charles Sanders Peirce
bio: American logician and founder of pragmatism who defined meaning in terms of practical consequences. Largely unrecognized in his lifetime, his work on signs (semiotics) and scientific inquiry proved foundational.

name: Richard Rorty
bio: American philosopher who argued that truth is a compliment we pay to beliefs that work socially, not a mirror of reality. He advocated irony, solidarity, and narrative over systematic philosophy.

name: Diogenes of Sinope
bio: Greek philosopher who lived in a barrel, begged for food, and told Alexander the Great to get out of his sunlight. His radical rejection of convention was a philosophical performance — every act was an argument.
key_ideas: the cosmopolitan as citizen of the world

name: Antisthenes
bio: Student of Socrates and founder of the Cynic school who argued that virtue alone constitutes happiness and that wealth, pleasure, and reputation are obstacles to the good life.

name: Crates of Thebes
bio: Wealthy Theban who gave away his fortune to practice Cynicism and became a beloved street philosopher in Athens. His marriage to Hipparchia — who also became a Cynic — was itself a philosophical statement.

name: Aristotle
bio: Student of Plato and tutor of Alexander the Great, Aristotle built a comprehensive system spanning logic, biology, politics, and ethics. His Nicomachean Ethics remains the cornerstone of virtue-based moral philosophy.

name: Thomas Aquinas
bio: Dominican friar who synthesized Aristotle's ethics with Christian theology. His Summa Theologica shaped Catholic moral philosophy, and his account of the virtues remains influential in secular ethics today.

name: Alasdair MacIntyre
bio: Scottish-American philosopher who revived virtue ethics in After Virtue, arguing that modern moral philosophy lost coherence when it abandoned the Aristotelian framework of practices and communities.
key_ideas: after virtue: the critique of modernity

name: Confucius
bio: Chinese teacher and philosopher whose sayings — collected in the Analects by disciples after his death — shaped East Asian civilization for over two millennia. He saw ritual and relationships as the path to virtue.

name: Mencius
bio: Confucian philosopher who argued that human nature is inherently good — we have innate moral sprouts that need cultivation, not correction. His optimistic ethics influenced Neo-Confucianism and East Asian education.

name: Zhu Xi
bio: Neo-Confucian philosopher who systematized Confucian thought into a comprehensive metaphysics. His commentaries on the Four Books became the official curriculum for Chinese imperial exams for 600 years.
key_ideas: self-cultivation as the path to sagehood
